reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Consistent 'When to use / When to skip' pattern for every guardrail type — this is the most decision-supportive element on the page and exactly what a first-time user needs before enabling a policy.
Realistic error message examples (the fenced output blocks) set concrete expectations for what failures look like without requiring AWS knowledge.
The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, AWS behavioral questions (SCPs, CloudFormation limits), and choosing/comparison questions.
The 'Combining guardrails' table provides a practical production policy template that a team can copy and adapt directly.
Page scope is well-disciplined: covers the five deployment-centric guardrail types and links to sibling pages for the rest, rather than stuffing all 15 types into one page.
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript defineConfig blocks.
problems: CRITICAL FACTUAL CONFLICT: The FAQ states 'They are not defined in your stacktape.ts configuration file — they are fetched from the Console API at the start of each operation,' but the source file is types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts — inside the Stacktape config types package. This location strongly implies guardrails are (or can be) defined in the stacktape.ts config file. The page never shows how to actually configure a guardrail, which is the most critical gap for a first-time user trying to get something running.

The Warning box claims that debug:logs, info:stack, and init 'do not load guardrails' — this is a specific Stacktape behavioral claim not supported by any provided source file. A first-time user acting on this claim could be misled about guardrail enforcement scope.

The FAQ answer 'Despite being grouped under Database restrictions in the Console' is a Console UI navigation claim with no console-app source provided in this page's file list. Console UI claims require console-app source grounding.

The blockedCommands example mentions 'rollback' as a blockable command, but the git status shows cf-rollback.mdx in CLI docs, suggesting the actual command name may be cf:rollback — using the wrong command name in an example is a practical trap for users.
Guardrail types require-dead-letter-queue, require-waf, and require-custom-domain appear in the source union but are not mapped to any named sibling page in the Info callout, leaving a coverage gap.

The page has no concrete setup path — a first-time user reading every section still cannot answer 'how do I actually add a guardrail?' This is the single biggest practical gap for the stated audience.
mandatoryFixes: UNSUPPORTED CONSOLE-ONLY CLAIM: The FAQ (and implicitly the rest of the page) asserts guardrails 'are not defined in your stacktape.ts configuration file.' The source file types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts is squarely inside the config types package, contradicting this. If guardrails belong in stacktape.ts, add a <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[{label: 'TypeScript', ...}]} /> example showing at minimum one guardrail (e.g., stage-restriction or region-restriction) defined in defineConfig. If they are genuinely Console-only, the claim must be narrowed to cautious language ('Guardrails are typically managed via the Stacktape Console; the config types are available if your workflow requires defining them in code') and the contradiction must be acknowledged rather than hidden.

UNGROUNDED COMMAND-SCOPE CLAIM IN WARNING BOX: The Warning states that debug:logs, info:stack, and init do not enforce guardrails. This is a specific runtime behavioral claim about the Stacktape CLI not grounded in the provided source files. Either remove the Warning entirely (the scoped list of enforcing commands in the opening section is sufficient context) or ground the exclusions in a referenced source file. As written, first-time users may incorrectly infer that guardrail enforcement is narrower than it actually is.
optionalImprovements: If guardrails are configurable in stacktape.ts (which the source location implies), add a minimal working <CodeBlock intellisense> example early in the page — this alone would raise practicalUsefulness by 2+ points for the target audience.

Verify the command name used in the blockedCommands example: git history shows cf-rollback.mdx, suggesting the CLI command is cf:rollback rather than rollback. Update the example to use the exact command string a user would type.

The Info callout mentions three sibling pages but the source union has 15 guardrail types; require-dead-letter-queue, require-waf, and require-custom-domain do not appear in any named sibling page. Either extend the Info callout to name where those live, or add a brief one-line table mapping all 15 types to their respective pages.

Remove the Console UI grouping detail ('grouped under Database restrictions in the Console') from the FAQ answer for resource-type restriction — it is ungrounded and risks misleading users if the Console UI changes.

The 'Combining guardrails' section mentions 'if multiple guardrails fail, the first violation is reported' — this is an important behavior detail that should move into the 'How deployment guardrails work' section near the top rather than being buried at the end of the combining section.

Add a brief note on how to disable or temporarily bypass a guardrail (e.g., for a one-off emergency delete) — first-time users who set up the delete block guardrail will immediately wonder what happens when they genuinely need to delete a stack.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Clear conceptual framing in the opening paragraph: guardrails are preventive policies, not reactive monitors — the contrast with alarms is stated immediately and the page doesn't conflate the two.
Consistent 'When to use / When to skip' pattern for each guardrail gives engineers enough context to decide without just reading a property definition.
Combining guardrails table is the most practical section on the page — shows a realistic production policy in one scannable view with purpose annotations.

FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, choosing/comparison). The SCP vs guardrail answer is particularly useful for engineers evaluating defense-in-depth strategies.
No forbidden patterns: no fenced YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig import, no defineConfig in fenced TypeScript blocks, no marketing language.

Scope is correctly bounded by the Info callout — the page covers the five 'deployment' guardrail types and points to sibling pages for the rest, preventing the page from becoming an overwhelming reference dump.
H2 headings are concise noun-form labels, consistent with the style guide.
problems: Error message format strings (e.g., 'Stage "test-123" is not allowed. Allowed stages: production, staging, dev.') are presented as authoritative runtime output but do not appear anywhere in the provided source files. An engineer building CI error-handling or alerting automation on these exact strings would be working from invented facts.

Claim that guardrails are evaluated during 'deploy, delete, preview-changes, and codebuild:deploy' is not grounded in the provided source. The source file (guardrails.d.ts) defines guardrail types and properties only — it says nothing about which CLI commands load or evaluate them. If this list is wrong, engineers will have a false sense of security about what commands bypass the guardrails.

The FAQ answer 'Despite being grouped under "Database restrictions" in the Console' is a Console UI navigation claim. The provided source list includes no console-app files, so this is an unsupported claim about Console screen layout. An engineer looking for resource-type restriction in the Console would be sent to the wrong section.

The statement 'Guardrails run on every deployment in the organization, regardless of who triggers it' and 'There is no per-project or per-stage override' are architectural claims not present in the source types. These may be true but are ungrounded — if they're wrong, engineers will design policy workflows incorrectly.

The require-dead-letter-queue guardrail is present in the GuardrailType union in source but the Info callout mentions only 'Security and data protection', 'Resource limits', and 'Database guardrails' as sibling pages. It is unclear which sibling page covers require-dead-letter-queue, leaving engineers unable to find it.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify the invented error message format strings. The source files do not define runtime error message text. Replace the illustrative error blocks with a description of what the error conveys (e.g., 'The error names the violating value and the configured threshold or allowed list') rather than presenting a specific string as the actual output.

Qualify or remove the list of commands that evaluate guardrails (deploy, delete, preview-changes, codebuild:deploy) — this is not in the provided source. Either ground it in a source file that was not provided (note that it is sourced from CLI source/Console behavior), or replace with 'commands that trigger guardrail evaluation are documented in the Stacktape Console' to avoid a false-authority claim.

Remove the FAQ claim 'Despite being grouped under "Database restrictions" in the Console' — this is an unsupported Console UI claim and no console-app source was provided for this page. Replace with neutral wording ('The resource-type restriction guardrail accepts any Stacktape resource type identifier') without naming a Console screen location.

Add require-dead-letter-queue to the Info callout's sibling page references or explicitly state which guardrail category page covers it. Currently engineers looking for that guardrail have no navigational path from this page.
optionalImprovements: The 'How deployment guardrails work' section could note the order of evaluation when multiple guardrails are active — does it fail-fast on the first violation or collect all violations? The current text says 'the first violation is reported' (in Combining guardrails), but this is buried. Move this behavior statement earlier, into the How it works section, where engineers making deployment pipeline decisions would look for it.

Consider adding a one-line note on guardrail propagation timing — when a guardrail is added or changed in the Console, do in-flight or queued deployments get the updated policy? This is a gap engineers running automated pipelines will hit.

The resource type identifier list in the FAQ is useful but could drift out of date as new resource types are added. Consider linking to a canonical resource type list in the Stacktape docs rather than enumerating them inline, to reduce maintenance burden.

The 'When to skip' guidance for Stage restriction says 'Enable this guardrail once your team settles on a stage naming convention'. This is advice about timing, not about conditions for skipping — it doesn't help an engineer who genuinely should not use stage restriction at all. Revise to clearly describe the no-need case (e.g., a solo developer with no team policy requirements).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms.
The page is easy to scan: short H2s, repeated when-to-use/when-to-skip subsections, and a comparison-style table for combined policies.
The core guardrail property names from the source are covered: allowedStages, allowedRegions, blockedCommands, blockedResourceTypes, and maxResources.
The FAQ has useful breadth across Stacktape-specific, AWS/comparison, and operational questions.
problems: Many Stacktape-specific behavior claims are not grounded in the only provided source file, types/stacktape-config/guardrails.d.ts. The source defines guardrail types and properties, but does not prove Console management, organization-level scope, command evaluation timing, AWS API timing, CloudFormation changeset behavior, error message text, or first-violation reporting.
The page invents exact error messages for every guardrail. The source file only contains JSDoc descriptions of properties and examples of property values, not runtime error formats.

The command restriction section is internally risky: it says blockedCommands can include rollback, then warns that only deploy/delete/preview-changes/codebuild:deploy load and evaluate guardrails. The source only says blockedCommands are commands that are blocked, with examples delete and rollback; it does not establish the enforcement command list.

Console workflow and storage claims are unsupported. The page says guardrails are managed in the Stacktape Console, fetched from the Console API, grouped under Database restrictions, and take effect immediately, but no console-app or API source is provided.

The FAQ lists many blockedResourceTypes identifiers that are not visible in the provided source. The source says blockedResourceTypes is StpResourceType and gives only open-search-domain and redis-cluster as examples.

Several operational claims may be true but are not source-grounded for this page: no per-project or per-stage override, all guardrails evaluated together, already-deployed stacks continue running, and CloudFormation's 500-resource limit.

The page is practical as a policy guide, but it lacks any grounded example of what a guardrail definition object looks like. Since this is not Stacktape config, a small JSON-like policy example could help if the source supports the shape.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or narrow unsupported runtime/enforcement claims unless additional source files are provided. Specifically revise claims about Console management, organization scope, exact command enforcement list, pre-AWS timing, changeset creation, resource modification, immediate effect, and first violation reporting.
Replace exact error message blocks with cautious prose, or provide runtime source proving those strings. The current examples read like authoritative output but are unsupported by guardrails.d.ts.

Fix the command restriction section so it only states what the source proves: command-restriction has blockedCommands?: string[] and examples include delete and rollback. Do not claim a specific set of commands load/evaluate guardrails without CLI source.
Remove unsupported Console UI/location claims, including the Database restrictions grouping and Console API fetch behavior, unless console-app or server source is added.

Remove the unsupported full list of StpResourceType identifiers or replace it with the source-grounded examples open-search-domain and redis-cluster plus cautious wording that blockedResourceTypes accepts Stacktape resource type identifiers.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact table near the top with guardrail type, property, and source-grounded purpose for the five deployment guardrails covered on this page.
Keep the when-to-use/when-to-skip pattern, but shorten repeated wording where it relies on speculative organizational behavior.
If additional CLI/runtime sources are available, use them to reintroduce timing, enforcement, and error-message details accurately.
Consider adding a short distinction table for guardrails vs alarms: preventive policy vs reactive monitoring, with only claims supported by sources or the linked alarms page.

verifierResults.summary: The draft correctly identifies the deployment-related guardrail property names that appear in guardrails.d.ts, but it makes many runtime, Console, enforcement, and error-message claims that are not supported by the provided source. The only source file defines guardrail types and property shapes; it does not document how guardrails are stored, fetched, evaluated, or reported at runtime.

issues.statement: "Stacktape evaluates them at the start of every deploy, delete, preview-changes, and codebuild:deploy command — before any AWS API calls are made. When a check fails, the operation stops immediately... No CloudFormation changeset is created, no resources are modified."
issues.reason: The provided source only defines guardrail types and property schemas. It does not describe CLI enforcement timing, which commands evaluate guardrails, AWS API call ordering, CloudFormation changeset behavior, or mutation guarantees.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow this section to the sourced fact that these guardrails define restrictions for stages, regions, commands, resource types, and resource count. Remove or qualify runtime enforcement details unless command/runtime source files are provided.

issues.statement: "Guardrails run on every deployment in the organization, regardless of who triggers it or which project is targeted. There is no per-project or per-stage override — if a guardrail is active, it applies everywhere within the organization."
issues.reason: The source does not define organization scope, project scope, stage-specific overrides, user-trigger behavior, or override support. This would materially affect how readers design governance policies.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the organization-wide/no-override claim, or replace it with a cautious statement that the provided guardrail definitions are policy shapes and do not show per-project or per-stage override fields.

issues.statement: "Command restriction applies to the deploy, delete, preview-changes, and codebuild:deploy commands — these are the commands that load and evaluate guardrails. Commands that do not load global config (like debug:logs or info:stack) are not subject to command restriction guardrails."
issues.reason: The command restriction source only says blockedCommands is a list of commands that are blocked. It does not enumerate which CLI commands load or enforce guardrails, and it does not exempt debug, info, or init commands.

issues.statement: "Guardrails are managed in the Stacktape Console at the organization level. They are not defined in your stacktape.ts configuration file — they are fetched from the Console API at the start of each operation. This means guardrail changes take effect immediately for all deployments in the organization..."
issues.reason: The provided source contains no Console, API-fetching, persistence, immediacy, or configuration-location behavior. These are operational claims outside the type definitions.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Console/API/immediate-effect assertions unless console-app or command source files are included. A safe version can say that the provided guardrail definitions describe policy properties such as `allowedStages`, `allowedRegions`, `blockedCommands`, and `maxResources`.

issues.statement: The draft gives exact runtime error messages for stage, region, command, resource type, and resource count violations, such as `Stage "test-123" is not allowed. Allowed stages: production, staging, dev.`
issues.reason: The source documents property meanings but does not define error text, error formatting, whether all allowed values are printed, or whether resource names are included in violations.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace exact error-message examples with cautious prose: for example, "A deployment targeting a stage outside `allowedStages` is blocked." Do not show quoted runtime messages unless runtime source provides them.

issues.statement: "If multiple guardrails fail, the first violation is reported and the operation stops."
issues.reason: The source defines the union of available guardrail definitions but does not specify evaluation order, whether validation short-circuits, or whether multiple violations are collected.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the supported additive idea only if phrased from the policy perspective, such as "You can define multiple guardrail types." Remove the first-violation/short-circuit claim unless validator runtime source is provided.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms.

issues.statement: "Resource-type and resource-count guardrails are evaluated only when a configuration file is present."

issues.suggestedFix: Remove the conditional evaluation sentence unless validator source is provided. Keep the sourced facts: `blockedResourceTypes` blocks listed resource types and `maxResources` sets the maximum number of resources per stack.

issues.statement: "The type definitions include 15 guardrail types. This page focuses on stage, region, command, resource-type, and resource-count restrictions."

issues.suggestedFix: Either keep the expansion but make the intro explicit that the page also covers two adjacent deployment-shape guardrails from `GuardrailDefinition`, or move resource-type and resource-count coverage to a broader guardrails overview/resource-limits page if that better matches the intended route.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Opens with a self-contained 50-word paragraph that correctly distinguishes guardrails (preventive, pre-deploy) from alarms (reactive, runtime) — well-suited for AEO extraction.
The upfront summary table maps guardrail type → key property → purpose in one glance; best scannability decision on the page.
Every guardrail section has concrete 'When to use' and 'When to skip' guidance — opinionated and actually useful for decision-making, not just configuration.
FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, underlying AWS service questions (SCPs, IAM, CloudFormation limits), and comparison/choosing questions.
The 'Combining guardrails' table of layered policies gives a realistic production-policy blueprint that first-timers can copy directly.
All claims are accurately grounded in guardrails.d.ts — JSDoc examples (delete/rollback, open-search-domain/redis-cluster, maxResources: 20) are cited correctly and match the source.
The Info callout correctly acknowledges the other 10 guardrail types and cross-links to dedicated pages without derailing the page's focus on deployment restrictions.
No forbidden patterns present: no YAML resource blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no marketing language.
problems: Biggest gap for a first-time reader: the page explains what each guardrail does and why to use it, but never explains how to create one in the Stacktape Console. The reader is told guardrails live in the Console, then immediately left without any guidance on where to find the guardrails UI, what the creation flow looks like, or what fields to fill in. For a low-AWS-experience audience, this is the most likely point of abandonment.

The 'Combining guardrails' section is partially redundant. The prose in the opening two paragraphs restates what the individual guardrail sections already said. The FeatureComparisonTable that follows is the third time the same five guardrails are summarized (after the intro table and the individual sections) — adds noise rather than value.

A reader who wants to check optional fields (e.g. whether allowedStages is optional or required, or what AWSRegion values are valid) has no link to the full type structure.

The FeatureComparisonTable at the end of 'Combining guardrails' duplicates the top-of-page table almost exactly (same five guardrails, similar categories). A reader scanning the page sees the same content tripled and may wonder what they missed.

Add <ApiReference definitionName="GuardrailDefinition" /> (or the most specific subtype available in the generated schema) at the bottom of the page. If the generated schema key differs, use whichever definitionName covers the GuardrailDefinition union. This lets readers check optional vs required properties and valid values without leaving the page.

Trim the 'Combining guardrails' prose to one paragraph (the motivation for combining multiple guardrails) and remove the FeatureComparisonTable — it repeats the intro table. The cross-guardrail policy example table (Guardrail / Configuration / Purpose) is genuinely new and should be kept.

Replace the hand-authored StpResourceType identifier list with a note that the complete set is available via the API reference or the Resources documentation, and keep only the most commonly blocked types (open-search-domain, redis-cluster, batch-job) as illustrative examples. This prevents the list from going stale.

In the 'How validation works' section, clarify whether resource-type and resource-count guardrails are also evaluated during codebuild:deploy or only during the local CLI commands. The source is silent on this distinction and the page currently asserts it without a source-grounded basis.

The 'Combining guardrails' intro sentence 'You can configure multiple guardrails together; each guardrail expresses a separate restriction' is redundant with 'Combine multiple deployment guardrails to build layered policies' in the same paragraph. Collapse to one sentence.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms.
The page is easy to scan: compact H2s, comparison tables, concrete property names, and a broad FAQ help extraction and navigation.
Core guardrail properties from the source are named accurately: allowedStages, allowedRegions, blockedCommands, blockedResourceTypes, and maxResources.
The page gives useful decision support for stage, region, command, resource-type, and resource-count restrictions instead of only listing properties.
problems: Several operational claims are not grounded in the provided source file: Console configuration, organization-wide scope, Console API fetching, validation before specific commands, sequential evaluation, first-violation behavior, and exact error-message text are not present in guardrails.d.ts.
The FAQ repeats unsupported CLI enforcement claims, including that deploy, codebuild:deploy, delete, and preview-changes run validation and that debug:logs or info:stack do not trigger checks.
The page says guardrails are not part of stacktape.ts project configuration, but the source only defines guardrail types; it does not establish where guardrails are configured.

The resource type identifier section lists many StpResourceType values that are not visible in the provided source. Because the source only says blockedResourceTypes?: StpResourceType[], the full identifier list is ungrounded here.

The CloudFormation 500-resource limit, resource mapping details, orphaned-stack cost behavior, GDPR/HIPAA examples, IAM/SCP comparison, and regional pricing comments are useful context but not grounded in the provided source files.

The page claims the type definitions include 15 guardrail types, which is grounded, but then only documents five in depth while pushing the other ten to related pages. That is reasonable structurally, but the reader may expect clearer boundaries for what this page covers versus the full guardrail model.

Replace the ungrounded full StpResourceType identifier list with a narrower statement grounded in the source: blockedResourceTypes accepts StpResourceType values, and the JSDoc examples are open-search-domain and redis-cluster. Only list more identifiers if their type source is provided.

issues.statement: “Any attempt to run a listed command is rejected.” / “Blocking `delete` prevents anyone from removing a stack via the CLI.” / “The operation is blocked.”
issues.reason: The type source says `blockedCommands` are commands that are blocked, but it does not define enforcement mechanics, CLI scope, error behavior, or whether the block applies to every attempt by every user. The draft’s phrasing is stronger than the provided source supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Use narrower wording tied to the type definition: “Command restriction defines commands that are blocked, such as `delete` or `rollback`. Use it for commands your organization does not want allowed by policy.” Avoid claims about exact CLI enforcement flow unless CLI/validator source is provided.

issues.statement: “Resource type restriction blocks specific Stacktape resource types from being used in any stack… if any resource in the configuration uses a blocked type, the deployment is rejected.”
issues.reason: The type definition supports that `blockedResourceTypes` is a list of blocked resource types, but it does not state the scope “any stack,” nor the exact validation behavior “deployment is rejected.”
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to the supported shape: “Resource type restriction defines Stacktape resource types that are blocked through the `blockedResourceTypes` list.” Then explain the intended policy use without specifying validator behavior beyond what the source states.

issues.statement: “Resource count limit caps the total number of Stacktape resources allowed in a single stack… if the stack's resource count exceeds it, the deployment is blocked.”
issues.reason: The source supports `maxResources` as the maximum number of resources allowed per stack, but it does not specify the exact failure mode or deployment blocking mechanics.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the supported fact and soften the enforcement description: “Resource count limit sets the maximum number of resources allowed per stack through `maxResources`.”

issues.statement: “A single Stacktape resource often maps to multiple CloudFormation resources (a web service creates an ECS service, task definition, IAM role, and more), so CloudFormation's 500-resource limit can be reached well before your Stacktape resource count seems high.”
issues.reason: The provided source files do not define how a web service maps to CloudFormation resources, and this page’s source list does not include web-service implementation or CloudFormation synthesis source. This claim is specific enough to change how readers interpret `maxResources`.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the web-service mapping example or rewrite as general AWS background: “CloudFormation has its own per-stack resource limits; Stacktape’s `maxResources` is a separate Stacktape-resource policy threshold.”

issues.statement: “The property shapes described below correspond to the `GuardrailDefinition` union type defined in Stacktape's configuration types — each variant has a `type` discriminator … and a `properties` payload …”
issues.reason: The examples shown are accurate for the five variants on this page, but the draft phrase “each variant” could be read as a claim about every future or external guardrail variant. The provided source currently supports it for all 15 listed variants, but the wording is broader than necessary.
issues.suggestedFix: Use current-source wording: “In the provided type definitions, every `GuardrailDefinition` variant has a `type` discriminator and a `properties` object.”

issues.statement: “The `blockedResourceTypes` property accepts values from the `StpResourceType` union, which corresponds to the `type` discriminator on each Stacktape resource definition.”
issues.reason: The guardrail type file proves `blockedResourceTypes?: StpResourceType[]`, but it does not define `StpResourceType` or prove that it corresponds to every resource definition’s `type` discriminator. The claim is likely true, but not grounded in the provided source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to the provided source: “The `blockedResourceTypes` property is typed as `StpResourceType[]`; the JSDoc example uses `open-search-domain` and `redis-cluster`.”

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Opens with a self-contained 50-word paragraph that correctly distinguishes guardrails (preventive, pre-deploy) from alarms (reactive, runtime) — well-suited for AEO extraction.
The upfront summary table maps guardrail type → key property → purpose in one glance; best scannability decision on the page.
Every guardrail section has concrete 'When to use' and 'When to skip' guidance — opinionated and actually useful for decision-making, not just configuration.
FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions, underlying AWS service questions (SCPs, IAM, CloudFormation limits), and comparison/choosing questions.
The 'Combining guardrails' table of layered policies gives a realistic production-policy blueprint that first-timers can copy directly.
All claims are accurately grounded in guardrails.d.ts — JSDoc examples (delete/rollback, open-search-domain/redis-cluster, maxResources: 20) are cited correctly and match the source.
The Info callout correctly acknowledges the other 10 guardrail types and cross-links to dedicated pages without derailing the page's focus on deployment restrictions.
No forbidden patterns present: no YAML resource blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no marketing language.
problems: Biggest gap for a first-time reader: the page explains what each guardrail does and why to use it, but never explains how to create one in the Stacktape Console. The reader is told guardrails live in the Console, then immediately left without any guidance on where to find the guardrails UI, what the creation flow looks like, or what fields to fill in. For a low-AWS-experience audience, this is the most likely point of abandonment.

The 'Combining guardrails' section is partially redundant. The prose in the opening two paragraphs restates what the individual guardrail sections already said. The FeatureComparisonTable that follows is the third time the same five guardrails are summarized (after the intro table and the individual sections) — adds noise rather than value.

No <ApiReference> component anywhere on the page. The source defines a well-formed GuardrailDefinition union type with 15 members, each with a typed properties object. A reader who wants to check optional fields (e.g. whether allowedStages is optional or required, or what AWSRegion values are valid) has no link to the full type structure.

The StpResourceType identifiers list in the 'Resource type identifiers' subsection is hand-authored and will silently go stale if the source union changes. It also omits `custom-resource` and `deployment-script` which appear in the type definitions.

The FeatureComparisonTable at the end of 'Combining guardrails' duplicates the top-of-page table almost exactly (same five guardrails, similar categories). A reader scanning the page sees the same content tripled and may wonder what they missed.
optionalImprovements: Add a short Console walkthrough under 'Where guardrails are configured' — even two or three steps ('Navigate to Settings → Guardrails → New guardrail, select the type, fill in the values, save') would close the biggest gap for first-time readers who cannot figure out where to go after reading the page.

Add <ApiReference definitionName="GuardrailDefinition" /> (or the most specific subtype available in the generated schema) at the bottom of the page. If the generated schema key differs, use whichever definitionName covers the GuardrailDefinition union. This lets readers check optional vs required properties and valid values without leaving the page.

Trim the 'Combining guardrails' prose to one paragraph (the motivation for combining multiple guardrails) and remove the FeatureComparisonTable — it repeats the intro table. The cross-guardrail policy example table (Guardrail / Configuration / Purpose) is genuinely new and should be kept.

Replace the hand-authored StpResourceType identifier list with a note that the complete set is available via the API reference or the Resources documentation, and keep only the most commonly blocked types (open-search-domain, redis-cluster, batch-job) as illustrative examples. This prevents the list from going stale.

In the 'How validation works' section, clarify whether resource-type and resource-count guardrails are also evaluated during codebuild:deploy or only during the local CLI commands. The source is silent on this distinction and the page currently asserts it without a source-grounded basis.

The 'Combining guardrails' intro sentence 'You can configure multiple guardrails together; each guardrail expresses a separate restriction' is redundant with 'Combine multiple deployment guardrails to build layered policies' in the same paragraph. Collapse to one sentence.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes preventive guardrails from reactive alarms.
The page is easy to scan: compact H2s, comparison tables, concrete property names, and a broad FAQ help extraction and navigation.
Core guardrail properties from the source are named accurately: allowedStages, allowedRegions, blockedCommands, blockedResourceTypes, and maxResources.
The page gives useful decision support for stage, region, command, resource-type, and resource-count restrictions instead of only listing properties.

The FAQ repeats unsupported CLI enforcement claims, including that deploy, codebuild:deploy, delete, and preview-changes run validation and that debug:logs or info:stack do not trigger checks.

The resource type identifier section lists many StpResourceType values that are not visible in the provided source. Because the source only says blockedResourceTypes?: StpResourceType[], the full identifier list is ungrounded here.

The CloudFormation 500-resource limit, resource mapping details, orphaned-stack cost behavior, GDPR/HIPAA examples, IAM/SCP comparison, and regional pricing comments are useful context but not grounded in the provided source files.

The page claims the type definitions include 15 guardrail types, which is grounded, but then only documents five in depth while pushing the other ten to related pages. That is reasonable structurally, but the reader may expect clearer boundaries for what this page covers versus the full guardrail model.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or qualify unsupported implementation details about Console storage, organization-level scope, Console API fetching, exact CLI validation timing, sequential validation, first reported violation, and exact error messages unless additional source files are provided.

Replace the ungrounded full StpResourceType identifier list with a narrower statement grounded in the source: blockedResourceTypes accepts StpResourceType values, and the JSDoc examples are open-search-domain and redis-cluster. Only list more identifiers if their type source is provided.
Revise the FAQ answers about command enforcement and non-mutating commands so they only state what guardrails.d.ts supports, or add CLI source files that prove those behaviors.

Remove or clearly frame unsupported AWS and governance context such as CloudFormation’s 500-resource limit, SCP/IAM behavior, GDPR/HIPAA requirements, and regional pricing as general background only if the review policy allows it for this page; do not present it as Stacktape behavior.
For every corrected claim, replace it with the source-grounded fact rather than deleting the topic entirely: name the guardrail type and exact property from guardrails.d.ts.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact table covering all 15 guardrail types with their exact property names, then keep the deeper sections focused on the five deployment-oriented guardrails.

Add a short note that optional properties are shown with ? in the TypeScript definitions, and avoid implying that an empty or omitted property has a documented default unless a validator source proves it.
Consider replacing the raw example error block with a clearly marked illustrative example, or remove it unless CLI validator source provides the exact message.
